brief_document.title: Activities: typed Activity values, decorators, RetryPolicy and Backoff
brief_document.purpose: Make activities typed values. activity.new builds an Activity(input, output) whose type parameters flow into workflow.run; the retry/timeout/heartbeat decorators attach typed policy data in a composable pipeline; RetryPolicy and Backoff are typed config with no baked-in defaults.
brief_document.task: Define aion/activity: an opaque Activity(i, o) carrying name, typed input, output Codec, runner, and policy config. Define activity.new and the retry/timeout/heartbeat decorators (each returning a new Activity(i, o)). Define RetryPolicy and the Backoff sum type (Exponential | Linear | Fixed). Bake in no default policy — an undecorated activity runs once.

requirements.title: Activity(i, o) and activity.new
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define an opaque Activity(i, o) built by activity.new(name, input, run) carrying the name, typed input, an output Codec, and the runner fn(i) -> Result(o, ActivityError).
acceptance: activity.gleam defines an opaque Activity(i, o) type parameterised by input and output.

activity.new(name: String, input: i, run: fn(i) -> Result(o, ActivityError)) constructs an Activity(i, o); the output Codec is supplied (as an argument or via a labelled field) so the result can be decoded at the boundary.
The type parameters i and o are carried such that workflow.run(Activity(i, o)) can return Result(o, ActivityError) (verified once AF-005 lands; this brief establishes the shape).
A unit test constructs an Activity with concrete input/output types and reads back its name.

requirements.title: retry / timeout / heartbeat decorators
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define activity.retry, activity.timeout, and activity.heartbeat, each taking and returning an Activity(i, o) so they compose in a pipeline.
acceptance: activity.retry(Activity(i, o), RetryPolicy) -> Activity(i, o) attaches the retry policy.
activity.timeout(Activity(i, o), Duration) -> Activity(i, o) attaches the timeout.
activity.heartbeat(Activity(i, o), Duration) -> Activity(i, o) attaches the heartbeat interval for long-running activities.
Each decorator returns a new Activity(i, o) preserving the type parameters; a unit test composes new |> retry |> timeout |> heartbeat and asserts the config is carried.

requirements.title: RetryPolicy and Backoff
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define RetryPolicy (max_attempts + Backoff) and Backoff as a sum type with Exponential, Linear, and Fixed variants, with no default policy baked into the SDK.
acceptance: RetryPolicy carries max_attempts and a Backoff (and any further documented fields, e.g. non-retryable-error filter, expressed in terms of AF-003's classification).
Backoff is Exponential(initial: Duration, multiplier, max: Duration), Linear(initial: Duration, increment: Duration, max: Duration), Fixed(delay: Duration).

No constructor supplies a default RetryPolicy, default timeout, or default backoff numbers; a doc comment states that an activity with no retry decorator runs exactly once (CO8 / no-assumed-defaults).
A unit test constructs each Backoff variant and a RetryPolicy and asserts the values are carried.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement activity dispatch — workflow.run is AF-005. This brief defines the Activity value and its policy data; dispatch reads them.
SHALL NOT bake in any default retry/timeout/backoff value (CO8).
SHALL NOT declare an @external — the Activity value is pure Gleam data; the ffi dispatch is AF-005.
verification: gleam test passes for the activity construction, decorator composition, and policy tests.
No default policy values are present; no panic/todo in new code.

checklist.text: activity.new(name, input, run) builds an opaque Activity(i, o) carrying the name, typed input, output Codec, and runner fn(i) -> Result(o, ActivityError).
activity.retry, activity.timeout, and activity.heartbeat each return a new Activity(i, o), composing in a pipeline.
RetryPolicy carries max_attempts and a Backoff; no default RetryPolicy is baked in — an undecorated activity runs once.
Backoff is a sum type with Exponential, Linear, and Fixed variants carrying their typed parameters.

stories.text: As a workflow author, I want to compose retry, timeout, and heartbeat onto an activity in a readable pipeline so that the activity's policy reads as data next to its definition.

constraints.text: Pure Gleam package. No Rust, no engine code, no beamr dependency inside aion_flow. It publishes to Hex standalone (gleam_stdlib is its only runtime dependency, plus gleam_json for the default JSON codecs).
Every @external(erlang, ...) declaration lives in the single internal module aion/internal/ffi. No author-facing module declares an @external (per D1).

Module files contain logic; the package root and any mod-equivalent re-export aggregator stay declarations/re-exports only. No god files — split before a module exceeds ~500 lines of code (excluding comments/whitespace).

No partial implementations. Every public function is complete and total: no panic, no todo, no assert-as-control-flow; failure paths return typed Result errors. Gleam has no exceptions for control flow — every fallible binding returns Result.

Type safety is enforced, not advisory: activity input/output, signal payload, query return, and child input/output types are static type parameters; an invalid composition fails `gleam build`. A type-safety test brief asserts representative invalid compositions are rejected (compile-fail expectations documented).

Determinism: the SDK exposes workflow.now/workflow.random bound to AD's determinism context and exposes NO binding to a wall clock or entropy source. Workflow-code documentation states the prohibition explicitly.

The recorded/side-effectful boundary is structural: the only side-effect dispatch the SDK offers is workflow.run(activity) (and all/race/map over activities). There is no generic side_effect(fn) escape hatch (per D4).

No hardcoded defaults for configurable values: no default RetryPolicy, no default timeout, no default backoff numbers baked into the SDK. An activity without a retry decorator runs once; the author supplies all policy values (CLAUDE.md no-assumed-defaults).
User data crosses the boundary only through a Codec(a); a decode failure is a typed DecodeError, never a panic. No primitive silently swallows a decode error.

aion_flow does not implement engine mechanics (timer firing, signal routing, replay) — those are AE/AD/AT, reached via @external. It does not author NIFs in Rust — that is AN. It is not packaged into .aion — that is AP. The cluster owns only the Gleam API surface and its binding declarations.
All acceptance uses Gleam tooling: gleam build, gleam check, gleam format --check, gleam test must all pass clean. The test harness (aion/testing) runs under gleam test with no external services.

resolved_context.intention: Define aion_flow, the Gleam package (published to Hex) that workflow authors import to write durable workflows. It is the human-facing surface of the entire engine: a workflow author writes a plain, statically-typed Gleam function — decisions, loops, data transformation — and reaches the outside world only through this SDK's activity, signal, query, timer, and child-workflow primitives. The package is pure Gleam with no engine code inside it; every primitive is a thin @external(erlang) binding that resolves at runtime, when beamr loads the compiled workflow and the engine has registered the matching NIFs (AE-004), to the engine-side mechanics in AE/AD/AT. The defining property is compile-time type safety: activity inputs and outputs, signal payloads, and query returns are all statically typed, so an invalid composition — passing a String where an activity expects an Order, awaiting a signal of the wrong type, returning the wrong type from a query handler — fails at `gleam build`, not three days into a long-running production workflow. This is the concrete advantage over Temporal's SDKs (Go weakly typed, TypeScript better but runtime-checked). The package also ships aion/testing, the Temporal-equivalent test harness: simulate time so a month-long sleep resolves instantly, mock activities to return canned results, and assert that a workflow replays to the same state — all in plain gleam test, with no engine, no beamr, and no store.
